title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Glass Palimpsest: The Ethics of Gentrification
content.subtitle: Navigating the tension between urban renewal and social displacement.
content.text: Cities are never finished; they are palimpsests—documents that are erased and rewritten over generations. Today, this process is most visible in 'gentrification,' a term coined in the 1960s to describe the influx of middle-class inhabitants into formerly working-class neighborhoods. [31] While city officials often laud this as 'revitalization,' long-term residents frequently experience it as an invisible invasion that erodes the social fabric of their community. [/31] The gleaming coffee shops and renovated lofts are often built upon a foundation of rising rents and cultural erasure. || The economic logic of gentrification is straightforward. Capital flows toward 'undervalued' areas where the gap between current and potential property value is widest. [32] This reinvestment can bring much-needed improvements: better lighting, cleaner streets, and reduced crime rates. [/32] However, these benefits are often unevenly distributed. The very improvements that attract new residents are the ones that make the area unaffordable for those who stayed during the lean years. The 'neighborhood improvement' thus becomes a mechanism for exclusion. || [33] A common defense of gentrification is that it creates 'socially mixed' communities, breaking up pockets of poverty. [/33] Yet, sociological studies suggest that physical proximity does not necessarily lead to social integration. Newcomers and long-term residents often inhabit parallel worlds, frequenting different businesses and maintaining separate social networks. The result is not a 'melting pot' but a 'tectonic' neighborhood, where two different populations rub against each other without ever truly blending. || [34] Furthermore, the loss of a neighborhood’s 'soul' is often dismissed as mere nostalgia, but for many, it represents a genuine loss of social capital. [/34] Local institutions—the corner bodega, the community center, the dive bar—act as informal safety nets. When these are replaced by high-end chains, the 'weak ties' that hold a community together are severed. For an elderly resident, the loss of a friendly face at the local shop is not just an inconvenience; it is a profound isolation. || [35] Some cities are experimenting with 'inclusive zoning' as a way to mitigate these effects. [/35] This requires developers to set aside a percentage of new units for low-income housing. While a step in the right direction, these policies often struggle to keep pace with the sheer speed of market-driven displacement. The challenge is to encourage investment without sacrificing the people who make the city worth living in. || [36] Ultimately, the debate over gentrification forces us to ask: for whom are we building our cities? [/36] If a city becomes a playground only for the wealthy, it loses the diversity and friction that drive innovation and culture. A healthy city needs both the new and the old, the polished and the grit. Protecting the rights of long-term residents is not about stopping progress, but about ensuring that 'progress' does not become a synonym for 'eviction.'

questions.question: What contrast does the author establish in the first paragraph?
options: A) Between the historical meaning of gentrification and its modern use.
B) Between the official view of urban growth and the lived experience of residents.
C) Between the architectural styles of the 1960s and those of today.
D) Between the speed of city life and the slow pace of community building.
questions.explanation: The text contrasts 'city officials' who call it 'revitalization' with 'long-term residents' who see it as an 'invasion' that erodes their community.

questions.question: The author suggests that the physical improvements in gentrifying areas...
options: A) are usually of poor quality and do not last long.
B) are only implemented after the original residents have left.
C) act as a catalyst for the eventual removal of poorer inhabitants.
D) are paid for entirely by the new middle-class residents.
questions.explanation: The text states that these improvements make the area 'unaffordable' for original residents, turning improvement into a 'mechanism for exclusion.'

questions.question: What is the author's opinion on 'socially mixed' communities?
options: A) They are the most effective way to eliminate urban poverty.
B) They are difficult to achieve because residents of different backgrounds tend to stay separate.
C) They have been successfully implemented in most major global cities.
D) They are a myth created by developers to justify rising property prices.
questions.explanation: The author notes that proximity doesn't lead to integration and that different groups inhabit 'parallel worlds' and 'separate social networks.'

questions.question: According to the fourth paragraph, why are local institutions important?
options: A) They provide high-quality goods that chains cannot replicate.
B) They help preserve the historical architecture of the neighborhood.
C) They serve as essential support systems for vulnerable residents.
D) They are more profitable than high-end retail businesses.
questions.explanation: The text describes them as 'informal safety nets' and 'weak ties' that prevent isolation for people like the elderly.

questions.question: What is mentioned as a limitation of 'inclusive zoning'?
options: A) Developers are generally unwilling to follow the rules.
B) It is only effective in cities with very low property values.
C) It cannot compete with the rapid rate at which the market changes a neighborhood.
D) It focuses too much on low-income housing and ignores the middle class.
questions.explanation: The text says these policies 'often struggle to keep pace with the sheer speed of market-driven displacement.'

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the author argues that a city's health depends on...
options: A) its ability to attract as much wealth as possible.
B) the complete removal of grit and old buildings.
C) maintaining a balance between different social and economic groups.
D) prioritizing innovation over the rights of long-term residents.
questions.explanation: The author concludes that a city needs 'both the new and the old' and that progress shouldn't mean eviction, emphasizing diversity.
